scenario: A complex subprocess has 20 inner elements. Team wonders if to show it expanded (all inner visible) or collapsed (small icon).
question: Collapsed vs Expanded subprocess visualization?

options.text: BPMN supports both views with [+] expand marker: a subprocess can be shown as a small rectangle with the [+] icon at the bottom (collapsed view, for high-level diagrams) or as a large rectangle showing all internal activities and flows (expanded view, for detail diagrams). Both views represent the same BPMN XML definition -- switching between them is purely a visualization choice. In Web Modeler and Desktop Modeler, you can click the [+] icon to expand or collapse the subprocess inline. For a subprocess with 20 inner elements, using collapsed view in the parent diagram reduces visual clutter while the expanded view remains accessible for detailed inspection.

BPMN subprocesses support both collapsed and expanded visualization. Forcing only the collapsed view would eliminate the ability to model and edit the subprocess's internal flow in context. Expanded view is essential during the modeling phase when the team is designing the 20 inner elements, and both views are valid final representations depending on the audience. Camunda's Web Modeler and Desktop Modeler fully support toggling between the two views without altering the underlying BPMN XML or affecting process execution semantics.

Collapsed and expanded subprocess views are not two separate BPMN files or process definitions. They are two visual representations of the same embedded subprocess element within one BPMN XML file. The subprocess definition, including all its inner tasks, gateways, and events, exists in a single file. Viewing it collapsed or expanded is a display preference controlled in the modeling tool. The process engine always works from the same XML definition regardless of how it is displayed, so execution behavior is identical in both views.

a.text: Incorrect. Subprocess visualisation modes: Collapsed subprocess: Visual: Single rectangle. [+] marker (or similar) at bottom. Subprocess name shown. Use case: Top-level overview: hide complexity. Audience: stakeholders, high-level review. Print / share: simpler diagram. Expanded subprocess: Visual: Larger rectangle showing internals. All inner activities, gateways, events visible. Inner sequence flows shown. Use case: Detail view: developer / modeler reference. Editing: see what's inside.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
